ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic dumpling food product class; no specific Korean chicken dumpling (mandu) subclass exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EKM covers filled dough/pasta products including dumplings. No Korea-specific fried chicken dumpling code exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Korean chicken dumplings. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Korean chicken dumplings is approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 150 g, as commonly served at Korean restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken dumplings (fried) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with USDA FDC entry for fried chicken-filled dumplings
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Korean chicken dumplings exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for fried chicken-filled dumplings / potstickers (e.g., FDC ID 2103689 and related entries), adjusted for Korean seasoning profile (gochugaru, sesame oil, soy sauce). Sodium is elevated relative to plain dumplings due to soy sauce content. Values represent a best-estimate composite for this food category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplementary data for filled pasta / dumpling products
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean chicken dumplings (mandu). GI of 48 is estimated from the closest analogous food: wheat-based filled dumplings / dim sum items reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 (GI range 45–55 for boiled/fried wheat dumplings with meat filling). Fried preparation raises GI slightly relative to steamed; value of 48 selected as conservative mid-point for fried chicken-filled wheat dumplings. GL per serving calculated as (48 × 33.0 g carbohydrate in 150 g serving) / 100 = 15.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~720 mg per 150 g serving) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Korean chicken dumplings to occasional consumption and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation where possible, as a single serving can contribute approximately 31% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~48) and moderate carbohydrate load from wheat-flour wrapper; fried preparation adds fat calories
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include Korean chicken dumplings in moderate portions (3–4 pieces), pairing with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response, while monitoring total carbohydrate intake as a 150 g serving provides approximately 33 g of carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from deep-frying and chicken skin; trans fat present in small amounts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Korean chicken dumplings sparingly due to the combined burden of ~720 mg sodium and ~3.6 g saturated fat per 150 g serving, and should prefer steamed or pan-fried variants with minimal oil to reduce overall cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean Chicken Dumplings (한국식 닭고기 만두; 韩式炸鸡肉饺子) are a popular Korean-style fried dumpling made with a seasoned minced chicken filling enclosed in a thin wheat-flour wrapper, then deep-fried or pan-fried until golden and crispy. The filling typically combines chicken mince with garlic, ginger, green onion, soy sauce, sesame oil, and gochugaru (Korean chilli flakes), producing a savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic flavour profile. The exterior is crisp and blistered from frying, while the interior remains juicy and tender. In Singapore, this dish is served at Korean restaurants and food courts, often as a starter or side dish, and is closely related to the broader East Asian dumpling family that includes Chinese jiaozi and Japanese gyoza. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving (approximately 6 pieces) provides around 327 kcal, 15.8 g protein, 15.3 g fat, and 33 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is notable at approximately 720 mg per serving due to soy sauce seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low GI), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 15.8. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or those with wheat or soy allergies. It is generally considered halal when prepared with certified halal chicken and soy sauce.

What is the GI of Korean dumplings?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean chicken dumplings was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for fried chicken-filled dumplings with adjustment for Korean seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source is a primary approved source and the analogous GI food is well-documented.
notes: 1. Cuisine correction: the input JSON listed cuisine as 'Japanese'; this has been corrected to 'Korean' based on the food name, description, and restaurant context. The cuisine_tags in the input included both Japanese and Korean; Korean is the correct primary cuisine. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried chicken dumpling / potsticker entries (e.g., FDC reference data for filled fried dumplings) and adjusted for Korean seasoning profile. 3. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN) for wheat-based fried meat-filled dumplings; no direct measurement for Korean chicken mandu was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source data for this food category. 5. Halal status is marked true on the assumption that the restaurant uses halal-certified chicken and soy sauce, consistent with the Singapore context; this should be verified at the restaurant level. 6. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting small amounts from frying oil; this is not zero and does not require a zero-macro justification. 7. All -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg) reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted USDA source for this food type.
